Turning complex healthcare data into decisions people can actually use.

I’m Koushik Tripurari (KT), a healthcare data scientist who enjoys the hard part: untangling enrollment logic, validating claims data, designing REDCap workflows, and turning analysis into dashboards that make sense to real stakeholders.

Currently focused on Medicaid claims, enrollment continuity, dashboard storytelling, and population-health analytics at the University of New Mexico.

Data strategist cover

Not just a dashboard builder — a data thinker who checks the logic underneath it.

I work in the space where clean methods, real healthcare context, and communication have to meet. My best work usually happens when a messy Medicaid question becomes a structured analysis, a trustworthy result, and a decision-ready visual.

Understands claims structure, eligibility periods, utilization, billing concepts, and data quality edge cases.
Builds stakeholder-friendly outputs with metric cards, tables, interpretation panels, and meaningful visuals.
Comfortable moving between SAS/Python code, REDCap workflow design, and executive-facing narrative.
Strong fit for roles that need technical skill plus clarity, ownership, and domain understanding.

What I actually do with Medicaid data

Enrollment continuity

Gaps, overlaps, and churn logic

Built logic to stitch enrollment periods, detect coverage gaps, interpret overlapping COE spans, and compare raw versus cleaned enrollment behavior. In this kind of work, one date assumption can change the story, so validation matters.

Claims analytics

Cohorts, services, and utilization patterns

Worked with ICD-10, CPT, HCPCS, NDC, status logic, service windows, header-line validation, and pre/post setups. I enjoy turning claims complexity into usable cohorts and interpretable outputs.

Behavioral health

Postpartum mental health follow-up

Developed proposal logic and analysis structure around postpartum mental health-related ED visits, index event definitions, 7-day and 30-day follow-up, rurality, and extended neonatal outcomes such as NICU use and low birth weight.

REDCap design

Survey instruments and cleaner capture

Developed REDCap instruments at UNM to capture and standardize complex discharge data across diverse care settings. I like designing data capture so downstream analysis becomes easier, not harder.

Quality control

Trust before storytelling

Validated missing lines, default admit dates, denominator choices, join behavior, duplicate windows, and other edge cases before presenting conclusions. Good analysis starts with skepticism.

Visualization

Dashboards with explanation built in

My preferred dashboard style uses clear metric cards, key takeaways, graphs, and one consolidated table so people can understand both the result and the interpretation in one place.

Workflow lens

How I move from raw data to decision support

1Understand the question and define the cohort clearly.
2Check enrollment, claims structure, and data-quality edge cases.
3Build reproducible logic in SAS/Python/SQL.
4Translate results into visuals and concise interpretations.
5Leave stakeholders with a clear answer and a clear next step.

Decision-ready style

More than “pretty charts”

The goal is simple: show what changed, why it matters, whether the result is trustworthy, and what someone should do or check next.

What I enjoy most

When visuals clarify the logic

I especially like building outputs where the visual design supports interpretation — not just display. That’s when dashboards become useful, not decorative.

Experience highlights

Aug 2024 – Present

Data Scientist • University of New Mexico

Applying analytics and visualization to Medicaid data to support patient care and policy decisions. Current work includes claims and enrollment logic, behavioral health, maternal/postpartum topics, REDCap, and Tableau/Power BI style reporting.

Aug 2022 – Feb 2024

Research Assistant – Data Science • IUPUI

Built gesture-based visualizations, worked with NLP and sentiment analytics, and helped create synthetic datasets that were useful for correlation-vs-causation exploration.

Sep 2020 – Jun 2022

Data Engineer • Tata Consultancy Services

Worked on ETL, Snowflake, Azure, dashboards, recommendation systems, and large-scale data pipelines. This role built the engineering foundation behind how I think about scalable analytics.

Education

  • Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis — Master’s in Data Science (Aug 2022 – Dec 2023), CGPA 3.84
  • SASTRA University — Bachelor of Technology (June 2016 – May 2020)
Certification

Advanced Medicaid Data Administration

Completed the Advanced Medicaid Data Administration course through the WVU Health Affairs Institute, awarded January 21, 2025. This adds formal training support to my hands-on Medicaid claims, enrollment, and dashboard analytics work.

A few personal strengths

  • I like translating technical work into language people can use.
  • I’m happiest when a messy question becomes a clean framework.
  • I care about the denominator, the date logic, and the edge cases.
  • I enjoy work that mixes analysis, design, and real-world impact.
1500 ELO
Chess.com rating

Chess is my second analytics lab.

Every position is a mini decision model: limited information, patterns, timing, trade-offs, and a sneaky edge case waiting to punish overconfidence. In other words, it’s suspiciously close to healthcare analytics.

The 1500 ELO rating reflects my chess side: patience, pattern recognition, and calculated risk.

View my Chess.com profile — 1500 ELO strategy mode →
“I debug enrollment overlaps the same way I play chess: check the obvious move, then look for the hidden fork.”

My chess side keeps me patient, tactical, and calm when the first answer looks tempting but the better answer needs one more pass. Also, yes — I do enjoy a clean endgame and a clean join.

Beyond the résumé

What I’m like to work with

I bring curiosity, structure, and a bit of calm energy to projects. I enjoy collaboration, but I also like owning the details: verifying assumptions, cleaning logic, and making sure the final output feels both accurate and useful.

Outside the strictly technical side, I tend to like topics that combine learning and strategy — AI, public speaking, health policy context, and of course chess. If a problem needs patience, pattern recognition, and practical communication, I usually feel at home.

Short version: I’m the kind of analyst who wants the chart to look good, the code to hold up, and the interpretation to make someone smarter in less than a minute.

A quick personality snapshot

For the fun readers

Fun match: Shikamaru Nara energy

For the fun enthusiast trying to understand my work style through a character lens: I give off Shikamaru Nara energy from Naruto — strategic, calm under pressure, pattern-focused, and more interested in the smartest move than the loudest one.

  • Why the match works: I enjoy solving complexity by breaking it into structure and finding the move that makes the most sense.
  • Project style: I am usually most useful when a team needs clarity, logic, and a steady head.
  • Work vibe: Quietly analytical, dependable, and more interested in effective results than unnecessary noise.

In portfolio terms, that means I like turning messy data problems into clean structure, useful dashboards, and clear next steps.

If you’re hiring for healthcare analytics, Medicaid data, dashboard development, population health, or research-oriented data roles, I’d love to connect.
